coredata.dc:title: Cu nanoclusters-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for ratiometric and visualization detection of copper ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhi-Chao
Qi, Jian-Wen
Hu, Chun
Zhang, Li
Song, Wei
Liang, Ru-Ping
Qiu, Jian-Ding
coredata.dc:description: Copper is a highly toxic environmental pollutant with bioaccumulative properties. Therefore, sensitive detection of Cu2+ is very important to prevent over-ingestion, and visual detection is preferred for practical applications. In this work, we developed a simple and environmental friendly approach to synthesize hyperbranched polyethyleneimine-protected copper nanoclusters (hPEI-Cu NCs) with great stability against extreme pH, high ionic strength, thiols etching and light illumination, which were then conjugated to the surface of silica coated CdSe quantum dots (QDs) to design a ratiometric fluorescence probe. In the presence of different amounts of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence of Cu NCs can be drastically quenched, while the emission from QDs stayed constant to serve as a reference signal and the color of the probe changed from yellow-green to red, resulting in ratiometric and visualization detection of Cu2+ ion with high accuracy. The detection limit for Cu2+ was estimated to be 8.9 nM, much lower than the allowable level of Cu2+ in drinking water (∼20 μM) set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Additionally, this probe can be also applied for the determination of Cu2+ ion in complex real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visualization
Polyethyleneimin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe (QDs@Cu NCs)
Procedure for the real water samples
Synthesis and characterization of hPEI-Cu NCs
Synthesis and characterization of QDs@Cu NCs
Sensing application of the ratiometric fluorescence probe
The selectivity of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of hPEI-Cu NCs at different excitation wavelengths. Insets show photographs of hPEI-Cu NCs solution under ambient light (left) and illuminated by an UV lamp of 365 nm (right). (B) TEM image of hPEI-Cu NCs. Inset shows diameter distributions of hPEI-Cu NCs. (C) AFM image (4 μm × 4 μm) of hPEI-Cu NCs deposited on freshly cleaved mica. Inset shows height distributions of hPEI-Cu NCs along the line shown in (C). (D) XPS spectrum in the Cu 2p region of hPEI-Cu NCs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of hPEI-Cu NCs at different pH values. (B) Fluorescence intensity of hPEI-Cu NCs after adding various concentrations of NaCl in both pH 7.4 and pH 4 solutions. (C) Fluorescence intensity of hPEI-Cu NCs upon addition of 1 mM thiols (GSH, Cys and DL-Dithiothreitol) or 3 mM pyrophosphate. (D) Fluorescence intensity variation of hPEI-Cu NCs as a function of time under 380 nm excitation.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of (a) QDs, (b) Cu NCs, (c) QDs@Cu NCs and (d) mixture of QDs and Cu NCs at 380 nm excitation. Insets show photographs of QDs, Cu NCs and QDs@Cu NCs (from a to c) solution illuminated by an UV lamp of 365 nm, respectively. (B) FT-IR spectra of (a) QDs, (b) Cu NCs, (c) QDs@Cu NCs and (d) mixture of QDs and Cu NCs. (C) TEM image of typical silica coated CdSe nanoparticles. (D) TEM image of typical CdSe@Cu NCs nanoparticles. The Cu NCs were located at red dotted line areas.

Fluorescence responses of (A) the ratiometric fluorescence probe and (C) the Cu NCs probe to different amounts of Cu2+ at 380 nm excitation. (B, D) Comparison between the fluorescence colors of the ratiometric fluorescence probe solutions (B) and the Cu NCs solutions (D) after exposure to Cu2+ illuminated by an UV lamp of 365 nm. The concentrations of Cu2+ from left to right are 0, 2.2, 6.6, 22, 62 and 111 μM, respectively. (E) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F495/F625) changes of the ratiometric fluorescence probe in (A). Inset: a linear relationship of F495/F625 versus the concentration of Cu2+ over the range from 22 nM to 8.8 μM F495/F625 was the fluorescence intensity ratio of the ratiometric fluorescence probe at two emission wavelengths at 495 nm and 625 nm, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the ratiometric fluorescence probe towards various metal ions (44 μM for all of metal ions, red bars) and the subsequent addition of Cu2+ ions (44 μM, blue bars) to the solution. The inset images show the corresponding fluorescence colors of the ratiometric fluorescence probe towards various metal ions under an UV lamp. (B) Fluorescence responses of the ratiometric fluorescence probe towards various amino acids (44 μM for all of amino acid, red bars) and the subsequent addition of Cu2+ (44 μM, blue bars) to the solution. The inset images show the corresponding fluorescence colors of the ratiometric fluorescence probe towards various amino acids under an UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe and its sensing mechanism to Cu2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Cu nanoclusters-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for ratiometric and visualization detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Institute for Advanced Study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper is a highly toxic environmental pollutant with bioaccumulative properties. Therefore, sensitive detection of Cu2+ is very important to prevent over-ingestion, and visual detection is preferred for practical applications. In this work, we developed a simple and environmental friendly approach to synthesize hyperbranched polyethyleneimine-protected copper nanoclusters (hPEI-Cu NCs) with great stability against extreme pH, high ionic strength, thiols etching and light illumination, which were then conjugated to the surface of silica coated CdSe quantum dots (QDs) to design a ratiometric fluorescence probe. In the presence of different amounts of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence of Cu NCs can be drastically quenched, while the emission from QDs stayed constant to serve as a reference signal and the color of the probe changed from yellow-green to red, resulting in ratiometric and visualization detection of Cu2+ ion with high accuracy. The detection limit for Cu2+ was estimated to be 8.9 nM, much lower than the allowable level of Cu2+ in drinking water (∼20 μM) set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Additionally, this probe can be also applied for the determination of Cu2+ ion in complex real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, environmental pollution has become a serious problem because of the wide and long-term harm, which has acquired a serious concern around the world for decades. In particular, copper as a widely used metal has caught great attention. Copper is a significant and essential microelement for animals and plants at its low concentration levels, it is also an important cofactor for many protein enzymes in living organisms. However, it may exhibit high toxicity and can cause damage to the central nervous system and disorders associated with neurodegenerative diseases (e.g., Wilson's diseases and Alzheimer's disease) under overloading conditions [1]. High concentration of Cu2+ may also induce gastrointestinal disturbance as well as liver or kidney damage [2]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) limits the Cu2+ level in drinking water to 1.3 mg L−1 (∼20 μM). Because Cu2+-containing materials are widely used in electronic device production, industrial and agricultural processes, the Cu2+ contamination is a continuous problem and the development of sensitive and selective methods for detecting Cu2+ are highly desired as an important issue both in environmental monitoring and clinical research.

There are many methods for Cu2+ ion detection, including atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy (AAS/AES) [3,4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [5], electrochemistry [6], dynamic light scattering technique (DLS) [7], Raman scattering [8] and fluorescence [9–11]. Among these developed methods, fluorescence-based methods show the application potential in revealing trace amounts of analytes because of their sensitivity, simplicity and cost-effective instrumentation [12–14]. However, most of the fluorescence responses to Cu2+ were based on the single fluorescence quenching probe [15,16], which is usually compromised by some other factors, such as the concentration change of sensors, drift of light source or detectors, or environmental effects in complex samples [17]. Ratiometric fluorescence probes can avoid these problems and have attracted much attention recently [11,17,18]. The signal variation of the ratiometric fluorescence probe is easier to be distinguished by the naked eyes [18], and the intensity ratio of two well-resolved wavelengths can provide built-in correction for environmental interference and eliminate the fluctuation of excitation light intensity [19,20], which improves the accuracy of the quantification [21,22].

Recently, metal nanoclusters have attracted considerable attention because of their unique size-dependent optical and electronic properties which differ substantially from those of the corresponding atoms and bulk materials, exhibiting great potentials as unique functional building blocks in a wide range of applications, such as optoelectronic nanodevices, biosensors, nanoelectronics and novel catalysts [23,24]. So far, extensive studies have concentrated on the synthesis of luminescent gold and silver nanoclusters [10,16]. The ease oxidation of Cu has limited progress in the development of synthetic methods for copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs), especially in an aqueous medium [25]. Thus far, studies focusing on the synthesis and optical tuning of Cu NCs are still scarce primarily because of the difficulty in preparing highly stable and extremely tiny particles. It has been reported that Cu NCs can be synthesized by using DNA [26], amino acid [27], protein [28] and polymer [29–32] as capping agents. In particular, hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) as a positively charged polyamine that contains primary, secondary and tertiary amine groups, which has been used as polyvalence ligand to modify and stabilize metallic or metallic oxide/sulfide nanocrystals since the amine groups could interact strongly with metal atoms [10]. For example, Au nanoparticles [33] and Ag nanoparticles [34] have been successfully synthesized with hPEI as scaffolds. Similarly, hPEI can also provide scaffolds that interact with Cu atoms during the formation of Cu NCs [31,32,35]. However, the previous methods suffered from time consuming, high temperature heating operation as well as the usage of toxic reducing agents, which would prevent their further applications.

Herein, we reported the synthesis of stable Cu NCs by a simple and environmental friendly one-pot chemical reduction method at room temperature, which used hPEI as the capping scaffold and ascorbic acid (AA) as the reducing agent, and the resultant Cu NCs were very stable against extreme pH, high ionic strength, thiols etching and light illumination. Furthermore, we developed a simple method to prepare dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting Cu2+ ion. As shown in Scheme 1 , the hPEI-Cu NCs with Cu2+ recognition sites were covalently linked to the surface of silica coated CdSe quantum dots (QDs). QDs were employed as the reference signal while the hPEI-Cu NCs acted as the report signal. In the presence of Cu2+ ion, the hPEI can capture the Cu2+ ion to form cupric amine coordinates, blocking the charge transfer from the amino group to Cu NCs core [26,36,37], resulting in the fluorescence quenching of bluish-green emission of Cu NCs (λem = 495 nm), while the fluorescence of red QDs (λem = 625 nm) stays constant. Consequently, variation of the two fluorescence intensities displays the continuous color's change after addition of different concentrations of Cu2+, which can be easily observed by the naked eyes under an UV lamp, resulting in ratiometric and visualization detection of Cu2+. Furthermore, this Cu2+ ion sensor needn't modify any receptor units onto the probe, because the hPEI-Cu NCs possess abundant amino groups which can recognize Cu2+. The selectivity and sensitivity of this ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor to Cu2+ were carefully investigated, and the feasibility of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for Cu2+ determination in real water samples were also investigated.

Branched polyethyleneimine (MW = 10 K, 99%) was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Silica coated CdSe quantum dots (QDs) were purchased from Shanghai Xintong Bio Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Cupric nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), ascorbic acid (AA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid (HAc) and other reagents were obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade or better and were used without further purification and modification. All samples and buffers were prepared using deionized (DI) water obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system. Britton-Robison buffer (BR buffer) solutions from pH 4 to 11 were prepared according to standard protocols, and the pH values were measured with a benchtop pH meter (Leici, China). All detection experiments were conducted in 50 mM HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4) and all glassware was cleaned with fresh aqua regia (HCl/HNO3 = 3:1, v/v) before using.

In a typical assay, 40 mg hPEI were dissolved in 2 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min to facilitate the dissolution. Subsequently, the pH was adjusted to 5 with HAc and then 2 mL of 2 mM Cu(NO3)2 solution was added under stirring, after complexation reaction between Cu2+ and the amine ligands for 10 min, 7 mg of AA was added to the solution and kept stirring continuously for 8 h before using. All reactions were carried out at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe (QDs@Cu NCs)
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesizing QDs@Cu NCs probe, the obtained Cu NCs solution were ultrafiltered using a Millipore (30 K, molecular weight cutoffs) with 16000 rpm for 10 min and an equal volume of as-prepared Cu NCs were mixed with 150 μL as-used QDs in 10 mM borate buffer (pH 7.4) solution. Then, 26.4 μg N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was added and shaking for 2 h. Finally, the nanohybrid was separated from free EDC by using a Millipore (30 K, molecular weight cutoffs) with 16000 rpm for 5 min and redispersed in borate buffer before using.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the Cu NCs were obtained with an UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of the Cu NCs and QDs@Cu NCs were obtained with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected with a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were recorded in the ScanAsyst mode with a Multi-Mode 8 atomic force microscope under ambient conditions (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were conducted by using a VG Multilab 2000X instrument (Thermal Electron, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Tenson 27 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) at resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. All the experiments were performed at room temperature, and the pH was calibrated with a pH meter.

Cu(NO3)2 was used as the Cu2+ source during the sensitivity studies. A Cu2+ stock solution (0.1 M) was prepared, and various concentrations were obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. To detect the Cu2+, 50 μL of different concentrations Cu2+ were added to 80 μL of the obtained dual-emission fluorescent probe solution and diluted to 230 μL with HAc-NaAc buffer, and then the mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 10 min before measuring the fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The river water samples were obtained from the Ganjiang River in Jiangxi Province (China), well water samples were obtained from a village in Jiangxi Province (China) and industrial sewage samples were obtained from Jiangxi Chengju Pharmacy Company in Jiangxi Province (China). All of the water samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane then diluted to 5% with ultrapure water and the Cu2+ was detected according to the above detection procedures. To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method, quantities of the standard Cu2+ solution were added to the sample solutions for recovery tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of hPEI-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the hPEI is synthesized by ring-opening polymerization of aziridine [7]. The polymer contains ample amine motifs which can interact and stabilize many metal ions, making it a good template and protection agent for synthesis of metal nanocomplexes [10]. Based on this, we developed a method for synthesizing hPEI-protected Cu NCs (hPEI-Cu NCs) in one pot. Comparing to previous reported methods [31,32,35], the hPEI-Cu NCs in our method were synthesized in a mild condition with shorter time. Since the original pH of the hPEI molecules was approximately 9 due to the abundant amine motifs, hPEI may interact with Cu2+ to form stable hPEI-Cu2+ complexes that hinder the reduction of Cu2+ under basic conditions [10]. It was found that fluorescent nanoclusters can be hardly formed at pH 9 even with a strong reducing agent, such as NaBH4. Therefore, the pH was adjusted to weak acid before adding the reducing agent to facilitate the Cu NCs formation. After ascorbic acid (AA) reduction, the solution turned from colorless to bright yellow, and strong bluish-green emission was easily observed under UV illumination (Fig. 1 A, insert), suggesting the formation of fluorescent hPEI-Cu NCs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum illustrates that the hPEI-Cu NCs have a broad absorption band at 310 nm (Fig. 1A). This molecular-like optical transition is due to the quasi-continuous electronic energy band structure and quantum confinement effects of Cu NCs [38]. Moreover, there was no absorbance band around 560 nm arising from the surface plasmon resonance of Cu nanoparticles, indicating that the sizes of the as-prepared Cu NCs were smaller than 5 nm [26]. Like most luminescent metal nanoclusters, the Cu NCs also exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. As the excitation wavelength increasing, the fluorescence peak shifts to longer wavelengths and its intensity decreases rapidly (Fig. 1A). The Cu NCs showed a high fluorescence quantum yield of 7.9% by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as reference chromophore [39] (Fig. S1). To further confirm the formation of the Cu NCs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) characterizations were carried out. As shown in Fig. 1B, the TEM image demonstrates that monodispersed Cu NCs are mostly spherical dots which are well separated from each other with averaging sizes about 1.7 nm (based on statistical analysis of more than 150 dots, Fig. 1B inset), confirming the formation of small Cu NCs, where the number of Cu atoms in each nanoclusters is usually below 13 [30,40]. Fig. 1C is the typical AFM image of the monodispersed Cu NCs on freshly cleaved mica surfaces, and the corresponding height distribution is shown in Fig. 1C inset. Many dot-like compositions with cross-sectional heights of about 2.5 nm can be observed, and the topographic morphology with small spherical dots is comparable to our TEM data. The oxidation state of Cu was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1D). There are two intense peaks at 932.2 and 952.0 eV, which were assigned to the binding energies of the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons of Cu (0). Meanwhile, a shake-up can be observed at 942.0 eV, indicating minimal Cu (II) was present in this system, which was derived from the unreacted Cu(NO3)2. It's of great importance to mention that the 2p3/2 binding energy of Cu (0) is only about 0.1 eV away from that of Cu (I). Therefore, the valence state of the obtained Cu NCs most likely lies between 0 and + 1 [27]. Previous study also reported that atoms can have different oxidation states in clusters, depending on their positions inside the clusters [41]. The XPS spectrum of other elements, including O, N and C, is shown in Fig. S2.

In order to obtain the best fluorescence intensity, we optimized the concentration ratio of hPEI to Cu(NO3)2 (hPEI/Cu(NO3)2), the fluorescence intensity of hPEI-Cu NCs enhanced with the increasing concentration ratio of hPEI/Cu(NO3)2 and the fluorescence intensity reached stability when the ratio of hPEI/Cu(NO3)2 was 2:1 (Fig. S3A). An optimal ratio of hPEI/Cu(NO3)2 = 2:1 was employed to guarantee the repeatability of the hPEI-Cu NCs synthesis. Besides, the comparable fluorescence intensity of various batches of hPEI-Cu NCs indicated the good repeatability of the synthesis process (Fig. S3B). We then implemented a series of experiments to confirm the hPEI-Cu NCs were water-soluble and stable toward ambient environments. The fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs demonstrated little change from pH 4 to 11 (Fig. 2 A), indicating the as-prepared hPEI-Cu NCs were quite stable toward different pH values. Simultaneously, the hPEI-Cu NCs kept stable in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl (pH 7.4 or pH 4), and no obvious fluorescence decrease can be observed even in 1 M NaCl solution (Fig. 2B), suggesting that the hPEI-Cu NCs were highly stable toward highly ionic conditions. Moreover, it has been reported that thiol compounds [42] and pyrophosphate [43] had specific interaction with Cu2+, which may influence the fluorescence of hPEI-Cu NCs in this experiment. However, addition of 1 mM thiol compounds (GSH, Cys and DL-Dithiothreitol) or 3 mM pyrophosphate had almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity of hPEI-Cu NCs (Fig. 2C), which may be ascribed to the good protection layer (hPEI) around Cu NCs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensities of hPEI-Cu NCs involved no obvious decrease after exposed for 1 h under 380 nm excitation (Fig. 2D), indicating excellent photostability of hPEI-Cu NCs. The high stability of the as-prepared hPEI-Cu NCs under various conditions made them remarkably better than the previous reported Cu NCs [31,32,35]. Such extraordinary stability might arise from the shielding effect of the protective hPEI layer, whose electron-rich amine groups can bind to the copper core and thus protect the Cu NCs structure [10,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of QDs@Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As the nanoparticle assembly may endow the new materials with collective effects, the fluorescence spectra, FT-IR spectra and TEM were used to investigate the ratiometric fluorescence probe. The QDs show an optimal fluorescence emission wavelength at 625 nm with 380 nm excitation (Fig. 3 A, curve a) and a high fluorescence quantum yield of 27% by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as reference chromophore [39] (Fig. S1). When the bluish-green emissive Cu NCs (curve b) are coupled to the surface of the red QDs, the obtained ratiometric fluorescence probe shows well-resolved dual emission bands under a single wavelength excitation at 380 nm (curve c). It is worth mentioning that Cu NCs' emission of the ratiometric fluorescence probe has a 6 nm blue shift compared with that of as-prepared Cu NCs, while the Cu NCs' emission did not suffer this shift in the mixture of Cu NCs and QDs (curve d), further confirming the Cu NCs were covalently attached to the QDs via EDC rather than the simple mixture of Cu NCs and QDs. And this 6 nm blue shift may be caused by the activation effect of the EDC, during the Cu NCs activating process by EDC, the stabilizer hPEI may be partly destroyed and departed from the surface of Cu NCs, meanwhile, some Cu atoms would dissociate, resulting in the blue shift of the emission wavelength [19]. The corresponding fluorescence color of the ratiometric fluorescence probe under an UV lamp is shown in Fig. 3A (insert c) for a direct comparison with those of the red emissive QDs (insert a) and the bluish-green emissive Cu NCs (insert b). These results suggest the bluish-green Cu NCs have been successfully covalently conjugated onto the surface of the red QDs [18]. In addition, the typical FT-IR spectra were also collected and the results demonstrate that QDs show peaks at about 1634 and 1386 cm−1, indicating the existence of COO− (Fig. 3B, curve a), while the peak at 3449 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching mode. The FT-IR spectrum of the hPEI-Cu NCs is shown in Fig. 3B (curve b). Three peaks located at 1563, 1638 and 3452 cm−1 corresponding to the bending vibrations and stretching vibration of N–H were observed, which were attributed to the hPEI capped on the surface of Cu NCs [35,44,45]. The covalent attachment of Cu NCs onto the surface of QDs (QDs@Cu NCs) rather than simple mixture of Cu NCs and QDs were also confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Two peaks were clearly observed at 1642 cm−1 (Amide I CO) and 1558 cm−1 (Amide II C–N and N–H) in the FT-IR spectra of QDs@Cu NCs (Fig. 3B, curve c), indicating the successful attachment of hPEI-Cu NCs onto the surface of QDs by amide bond [46]. However, compared with the FT-IR spectroscopy of ratiometric fluorescence probe, we found that the mixture of Cu NCs and QDs didn't show the peak at 1558 cm−1 (Fig. 3B, curve d), confirming that Cu NCs have been covalently attached to the QDs rather than the simple mixture of Cu NCs and QDs.

The architecture and optical properties of the ratiometric probe are studied in detail. The TEM image indicates that the size of carboxyl functionalized QDs were around 35 nm (Fig. 3C), and the center cores shown in Fig. 3C are hydrophobic CdSe QDs [47]. It is important to note that the edges of QDs nanoparticles are very thin with uniform thickness from the TEM image. However, when the hPEI-Cu NCs were successfully conjugated to the surface of QDs to form ratiometric fluorescence probe, the edges of ratiometric fluorescence probe are comparatively rough comprised with many collective Cu NCs around the QDs (Fig. 3D), demonstrating the formation of the ratiometric fluorescence probe nanocomplex [18,48], and the TEM gave another powerful confirming that Cu NCs were covalently attached to the QDs surface via EDC rather than the simple mixture of Cu NCs and QDs. Moreover, considering that the size of the Cu NCs was only about 1.7 nm (according to Fig. 1B), which were far smaller than the QDs, no obvious change of the particle size could be observed from the TEM image of the hybrid spheres even the Cu NCs have been successfully linked on the surface of the QDs.

In order to obtain the optimal experimental conditions for the proposed method, we investigated the effect of pH, reaction temperature and time on the fluorescence of the detecting system. As shown in Fig. S4A, the sensing system demonstrates strong response to Cu2+ over the wide pH ranges from 4 to 11 except for the strongly acidic media (pH < 4), which may be attributed to the fact that the amino groups on the surfaces of the ratiometric fluorescence probe are well protonated in strongly acidic media and are thus unable to complex with Cu2+ to form fluorescence-quenching cupric amine moieties [49]. It should be noted that the relative fluorescence intensity (F495/F625) of the ratiometric fluorescence probe shows a little decrease in alkaline solutions, which may result from the fact that partial hydrolysis of Cu2+ in the alkaline media inhibiting the complex reaction between Cu2+ and the amines of hPEI-Cu NCs. To maintain a good selectivity and obtain high sensitivity, pH 4 was selected for further experiment. Meanwhile, the reaction temperature in this detection system is another key factor, because the temperature can influence the complex reaction between Cu2+ and ratiometric fluorescence probe. The results show that 37 °C is the best temperature for this sensing method (Fig. S4B). Furthermore, the effect of reaction time proves that this detection system was fairly fast which reached equilibrium in less than 10 min (Fig. S4C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing application of the ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the response of the ratiometric fluorescence probe towards Cu2+ was carried out to prove the working principle. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , in the absence of Cu2+, the ratiometric fluorescence probe shows two emission bands centered at 495 and 625 nm, which are ascribed to the emission from Cu NCs on the surface and QDs embedded in the silica nanoparticles under single-wavelength excitation at 380 nm, respectively. Upon addition of Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 4A, the bluish-green fluorescence intensity at 495 nm is continuously decreased, whereas the red fluorescence intensity at 625 nm remains unchanged. To reveal the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, fluorescence lifetime analysis of the ratiometric fluorescence probe before and after addition of Cu2+ were measured at the emission wavelength of λem = 495 nm. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in Fig. S5A, demonstrating that the quenching of ratiometric fluorescence probe by Cu2+ ions obeys a dynamic quenching mechanism [50]. The life times of the ratiometric fluorescence probe are listed in Table S1, which followed biexponential fluorescence decay. From the previous report [26], the emission of the obtained Cu NCs was derived from triplet excited states, which was similar to general characteristics of luminescent high-nuclearity gold (I) complexes that displayed a ligand-metal charge transfer. In other words, the electron-rich amino groups of hPEI can interact with the Cu cluster surface and directly donate the delocalized electron density to the Cu NCs core, which can largely promote the fluorescence. Such a charge transfer was believed to affect the excited-state relaxation dynamics (e.g., radiative vs nonradiative rates). Since surface ligands played a major role in influencing the fluorescence of Cu NCs, we inferred that the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric fluorescence probe by Cu2+ was due to the coordination of Cu2+ with the amino group of hPEI, which might block the charge transfer from the amino group to Cu NCs core, resulting in the fluorescence quenching [36]. To test the hypothesis, we added equal amount of ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) to the Cu2+-quenched ratiometric fluorescence probe solution, and the fluorescence of the solution recovered (Fig. S5B), indicating that the interaction between Cu2+ and the ratiometric fluorescence probe was indeed through coordination. Further detailed investigations on the underlying fluorescence quenching mechanism are still under way.

Owing to the changes in the intensity ratio of the two emission wavelengths, the continuous fluorescence color changes from yellow-green to red as demonstrated in Fig. 4B can be observed. Clearly, even a slight decrease of the green emission intensity leads to an obviously distinguishable color from the original background with the naked eyes. The advantages of the ratiometric fluorescence for visual detection can be confirmed by comparison with a single fluorescence quenching experiment, in which only a Cu NCs probe is employed for the detection of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, unlike the ratiometric fluorescence probe, it is hard to distinguish the fluorescence image of the pure Cu NCs probe from other images in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ by the naked eyes (Fig. 4D). The comparison clearly indicates that the ratiometric fluorescence probe is more sensitive and reliable for visual detection of Cu2+ than a single fluorescence quenching method, although the intensity of the green emission decreases at almost the same level.

The sensitivity of the detection performance of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for Cu2+ was subsequently evaluated. As can be seen from Fig. 4E, the intensity ratio of the two emission wavelengths (F495/F625) gradually decreases with increasing concentration of Cu2+ and the signal ratio shows good linearity (R2 = 0.99) with the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 22 nM–8.8 μM (Fig. 4E insert). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be around 8.9 nM (3σ), which was lower than or comparable to those of previously reported Cu2+ detection strategies (Table 1 ). Besides the good sensing performance, the advantages of the ratiometric fluorescence probe on eliminating environmental interference and light fluctuation favor them as promising optical probe in the quantification of Cu2+ ions with high accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence probe to Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F495/F625) of the probe were recorded in the presence of Cu2+ and other metal ions, including Cr3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, K+, Ti2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Ag+. As shown in Fig. 5 A, no obvious changes of the fluorescence intensity ratio and the fluorescence color were observed for other metal ions, compared with that obtained for Cu2+ ions. It has been reported that hPEI may interact strongly with Ag+ to form stable hPEI-Ag+ complexes under basic conditions [10]. However, Ag+ ion could not cause fluorescence quenching of hPEI-Cu NCs in our method, the reason can be deduced as follows: since the detection in our method was conducted at pH 4, which would weaken the interaction between hPEI and Ag+ obviously [10], resulting in the coordination capacity of Ag+ far less than Cu2+ with hPEI, so Ag+ barely blocks the charge transfer from the amino group to Cu NCs core. In addition, taking into account that amino acids in the biological system are capable of interacting with many metal cations and may interfere with Cu2+ sensing, several amino acids (Arg, His, Ser, Phe, Ala, Thr, Gla, Met, Asp, Pro, Lys, Cys, Tyr, Trp, Gly, Val and GSH) were also chosen for investigation. As shown in Fig. 5B, no significant interference in fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were observed after addition of the above substances into the ratiometric fluorescence probe solution. These results indicate that the ratiometric fluorescence probe shows high selectivity to Cu2+ against not only metal ions but also amino acids. Besides, the negligible interference from other amino acids suggests the potential utility of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for detection of Cu2+ in biological samples.

The excellent specificity combined with high sensitivity and fast response of the ratiometric fluorescence probe to Cu2+ suggests that our method might be directly applied to detecting Cu2+ ion in real samples. We applied a standard addition method to detect the concentration of Cu2+ in the river water samples from Ganjiang River, well water samples from a village and industrial sewage samples from Jiangxi Chengju Pharmacy Company. Three concentrations of the Cu2+ ion (2.2, 3.3 and 8.8 μM) were spiked into the water samples, the recovery values were in the range of 104.5–118.2%, 95.5–103% and 90.9–101.1%, respectively (Table S2), indicating that our proposed assay could potentially be applied to complex realistic samples.

In conclusion, we synthesized bluish-green emission Cu NCs by a simple and environmental friendly method at room temperature by using polyethyleneimine (hPEI) as the capping scaffold and ascorbic acid (AA) as the reducing agent. By conjugating Cu NCs onto the surface of QDs, we have successfully prepared a novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe (QDs@Cu NCs) for rapid and reliable detection of Cu2+ ion with high sensitivity and selectivity. In this method, QDs were employed as the reference signal while the hPEI-Cu NCs acted as the report signal. The Cu2+ can interact with amine groups of hPEI-Cu NCs on the surface of the ratiometric fluorescence probe, which blocks the charge transfer from the amino group to Cu NCs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. This method provides a built-in correction for environmental interference and exhibits significantly enhanced visual detection capability compared with the single fluorescence quenching probe. Besides, this ratiometric fluorescence probe has been successfully applied for detecting Cu2+ in real water samples, demonstrating great potential in environmental and biological sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21163014 and 21475056), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-11-1002 and NCET-13-0848).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.09.002.
